title: Size Select Redesign
pitch: A multivariate test on the PDP size selector pitting two directions against the original layout. Variant A collapsed sizes into a dropdown to reclaim vertical real estate and pull the CTA up. Variant B kept every size visible but tightened them into a consistent 5-column grid with a uniform swatch footprint regardless of how many sizes a product carried. The grid variant won: it delivered the space savings without hiding information behind an extra tap.
problem: The default size selector consumed unpredictable amounts of vertical space depending on how many sizes a product carried, which meant the layout shifted product to product and frequently pushed the add-to-cart button below the fold. There was also a real tension in the remediation: tightening the layout risked either hiding sizes (and adding a tap) or making the grid feel cramped, so the test needed to resolve that tradeoff directly rather than assume one answer.
hypothesis: A more compact size selector would bring the CTA closer to the decision point and improve conversion, but we didn't want to assume whether hiding sizes behind a dropdown or simply laying them out more efficiently would win. Running both variants against control would let the data decide whether space savings or visibility mattered more to customers.
